title: The Hasse-Weil zeta functions of the intersection cohomology of minimally compactified orthogonal Shimura varieties
speaker: Yihang Zhu
abstract: Initiated by Langlands, the problem of computing the Hasse-Weil zeta functions of Shimura varieties in terms of automorphic L-functions has received continual study. We will discuss how recent progress in various aspects of the field has allowed the extension of the project to some Shimura varieties not treated before.

proof_stages.label: From Hasse-Weil zeta functions to a trace-formula strategy
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls local Hasse-Weil zeta factors via point counting and Frobenius on etale cohomology, uses the modular-curve theorem of Eichler-Shimura as motivation, and explains Langlands's strategy of relating cohomological Hecke-Frobenius traces to automorphic spectral data through the Arthur-Selberg trace formula.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal Shimura varieties and Kottwitz's intersection-cohomology trace conjecture
proof_stages.summary: After setting up Shimura data, hyperspecial good reduction, orthogonal Shimura varieties, and the Baily-Borel compactification, the central conjectural identity is stated: Hecke-Frobenius traces on intersection cohomology equal an endoscopic sum of stable geometric trace-formula distributions.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary trace identities, known cases, and payoff
proof_stages.summary: The compact-support analogue replaces intersection cohomology by compactly supported cohomology and the full stable trace formula by its elliptic part, is known in important cases by point counting and stabilization, and later supplies the equality of leading terms; related known results without Frobenius and the implication for automorphic Hasse-Weil zeta functions are also reviewed.

proof_stages.label: Levi decomposition of both sides
proof_stages.summary: Morel's calculation expresses the left-hand intersection-cohomology trace as a sum over rational Levi subgroups of G, while the stable geometric side for each endoscopic group H decomposes over rational Levi subgroups of H, with the leading terms identified using the compact-support identity.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to matched Levi-endoscopic contributions
proof_stages.summary: The remaining proof is reduced to classifying most pairs of an endoscopic datum H and a Levi M' of H by Levi subgroups M of G, proving equality for each M-labeled collection of contributions, and proving that the total contribution of the unclassified pairs vanishes.

proof_stages.label: Archimedean comparison and sign determination
proof_stages.summary: At the infinite place, the left side gives truncated group cohomology of unipotent radicals from boundary coefficient systems, while the right side gives Harish-Chandra characters of discrete series on noncompact maximal tori, so the proof needs exact linear-combination identities and correct signs from comparing transfer-factor normalizations.

proof_stages.label: p-adic boundary terms and cancellation of bad terms
proof_stages.summary: At the prime p, boundary point-counting contributions on the intersection-cohomology side are compared with transferred local factors on the stable side, separating matching 'good' terms from 'bad' terms that must cancel once combined with the Archimedean signs.

examples.name: Eichler-Shimura modular curve prototype
examples.note: The modular curve case gives the model identity in which the Hasse-Weil zeta function factors through Mellin transforms of weight-two modular forms, motivating the general search for automorphic L-functions for Shimura varieties.
examples.engagement_reason: The modular curve is presented as the Eichler–Shimura prototype and its known zeta-factor formula is stated, but no computation or derivation is carried out on it.

passes.reason: A compactified modular curve is a standard infinite-family Shimura variety used as the motivating prototype, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Levi with a GL2 factor and a compact orthogonal factor
examples.note: For a Levi subgroup resembling a product with GL2 and SO(n-2,0), the GL2 part produces modular-curve-like point-counting terms while the SO(n-2,0) part produces 'bad' nonexistent-Shimura-variety terms, making the need for sign-driven cancellation explicit.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes this Levi form concretely, separating its p-adic contributions into GL2 modular-curve terms and bad compact-orthogonal terms and explaining the required cancellation.

passes.reason: The definite compact SO(n−2,0) factor is a boundary compact/zero-domain case with no associated Shimura variety, used as a bad contribution that must cancel.
the definite SO(n-2,0) factor is an extreme compact-signature boundary whose would-be Shimura contribution is a bad term that must cancel
the Levi GL2×SO(n−2,0) has a definite compact orthogonal factor at a boundary/wrong-signature case, producing bad nonexistent-Shimura terms that obstruct naive matching and must cancel
